extracts.extract: Causal inference is the process of determining causality. The most important thing to remember is that the heart of causality is manipulation. You have to change something in order to measure the effect of it. This lack of manipulation is the reason why correlation is not causation.
extracts.notes: Core principle: no causation without manipulation

extracts.extract: Good causal inference questions sound like experiments. If you can't think of an experiment that would help you answer a causal question, you probably can't answer it at all, even if you use a lot of advanced statistics.
extracts.notes: Practical litmus test for whether a causal question is answerable

extracts.extract: Randomized controlled trials are the gold standard for causal inference. But in the real world, they are often too expensive, too time-consuming, or too unethical. If you can't run an experiment, the next best thing is to use a quasi-experimental method, which involves creating a treatment and control group from an existing dataset without designing and running an experiment yourself.
extracts.notes: Establishes a hierarchy: RCT > quasi-experiment > regression

extracts.extract: Difference in differences is useful when you have two groups, one treatment and one control, and two points in time, one before the intervention and another after. Card and Krueger used this approach to compare employment levels in New Jersey, which raised its minimum wage, to nearby areas of Pennsylvania, which did not.
extracts.notes: Concrete example from economics applied to policy journalism

extracts.extract: Regression discontinuity design is useful when there is a cutoff in some continuous variable that results in very different outcomes for observations near the cutoff, even though all the observations around the cutoff are fairly similar. By comparing observations just above and just below the cutoff, we can measure a causal effect.
extracts.notes: Illustrated with incumbency advantage example using vote share cutoff at 50%

extracts.extract: Regressions are often used to illustrate the relationship between two variables. But causality can't be measured without manipulation, so while a regression can help test a causal hypothesis by showing whether data is consistent with it, it can't on its own prove causality. Even when you're controlling for a lot of variables, you're really just doing a more advanced correlation.
extracts.notes: Key warning against over-interpreting regression results

extracts.extract: You should be extremely skeptical of drawing conclusions from correlations. You cannot make up for bad causal inference methods with more data, more code, more graphics, or more credentials.
extracts.notes: Concluding warning; the 'more data' point pushes back against big-data solutionism

extracts.extract: The Data Unit Guide is an organizational guide to data journalism for small-to-medium and non-profit newsrooms, covering modules on assembling and developing teams, integrating data units within newsroom hierarchies, choosing software and platforms, demonstrating and quantifying contributions, and securing sustainable funding.
extracts.notes: Module-level summary; original is a full standalone guide at dug.news

extracts.extract: Git scraping uses GitHub Actions to automate tracking changes to a scraped data source over time, providing a version-controlled record of how data evolves.
extracts.chapter: Git Scraping: Tracking Changes to a Scraped Data Source Using GitHub Actions
extracts.notes: Simon Willison lightning talk; git history serves as both acquisition log and change-detection mechanism
